articles.title: Evaluation order and nontermination in query languages
articles.summary: Evaluation order and nontermination in query languages are crucial aspects of functional programming that can lead to complex evaluation orders. A finite function can be represented as a key-value table whose keys are the function's support, which are inputs with non-default outputs. Non-boolean finite maps arise naturally from aggregations, while tensor algebra is a special case of relational algebra over these finite maps. The difficulty in evaluating such queries lies in determining their evaluation order, which can lead to nontermination and unexpected behavior.

articles.title: What happens when you run a CUDA kernel?
articles.summary: A CUDA kernel is executed on a graphics processing unit (GPU) using the Compute Unified Device Architecture (CUDA). The key technology involved is the use of massively parallel processing to perform computations in parallel. This allows for significant performance gains compared to traditional CPU-based applications. The process involves compiling the code into a virtual instruction set, which is then translated into machine code that the GPU can execute.

articles.title: Optimizing LLVM's bump allocator
articles.summary: The Optimizing LLVM's bump allocator, which optimizes memory allocation by bumping pointers within a slab and freeing everything at once when the allocator dies, has undergone three recent changes that streamline its performance. articles.title: You might not need… a service worker
articles.summary: Service workers can be useful for caching certain types of data, but they may not always be necessary. In the example given, Slack's use of service workers to cache assets like JavaScript files resulted in stale downloads even after a network request had resolved. This suggests that relying on service workers alone may not provide sufficient offline support for all applications. Instead, developers can consider using content hashing and caching static assets separately from dynamic data, which would eliminate the need for service workers altogether.

articles.title: HackerRank open sourced its ATS. My resume scored 90/100. Oh wait 74. No – 88
articles.summary: HackerRank's open-sourced ATS has been blowing up with hundreds to thousands of likes on LinkedIn and Reddit, but it's not working as intended for developers like you. The tool's scoring system is based on parsing a resume into text, extracting structured information, and feeding it into an LLM (large language model) that grades the score out of 100. While technical skills are a checklist, they're easily judged by an LLM, resulting in inconsistent scores across different projects. The temperature setting has little impact, and even going down to 0 hasn't fixed the issue. This means that hiring is becoming more of a luck filter than a skill-based process, with developers like you being left out of the loop when it comes to finding job opportunities.

articles.title: Ante: New Way to Blend Borrow Checking and Reference Counting

This is promising because it means that someday we can more easily combine them in flexible ways without risking run-time errors. Ante has found a way to do this for mutable objects, which is close to the same reason I work on Vale. However, Ante's design still needs refinement and testing before it can be widely adopted.
